question_text: According to ICAO Annex 14 Volume I, what is the purpose of the specifications contained in this Annex?

options.A: To regulate the operation of aircraft at aerodromes
options.B: To limit the growth of obstacles around aerodromes only
options.C: To prescribe physical characteristics and obstacle limitation surfaces for aerodromes, and certain facilities and technical services normally provided
options.D: To provide economic and environmental planning guidance for aerodromes
options.E: To set standards for air traffic control procedures only
options.F: To define aircraft performance characteristics for take-off

explanation: Chapter 1, General, Introductory Note states: 'This Annex contains Standards and Recommended Practices (specifications) that prescribe the physical characteristics and obstacle limitation surfaces to be provided for at aerodromes, and certain facilities and technical services normally provided at an aerodrome.'

question_text: True or False: The specifications in Annex 14 Volume I are intended to limit or regulate the operation of aircraft.
explanation: Chapter 1, General, Introductory Note explicitly states: 'It is not intended that these specifications limit or regulate the operation of an aircraft.'

question_text: For which categories of precision approach runways are the specifications in Annex 14 Volume I applicable only to runways intended to be used by aeroplanes in code numbers 3 and 4?
explanation: Chapter 1, General: 'It is to be noted that the specifications for precision approach runway categories II and III are only applicable to runways intended to be used by aeroplanes in code numbers 3 and 4.'

question_text: According to Chapter 1, what is the definition of an 'Aerodrome'?

options.A: Any area of land designed for the take-off and landing of aircraft
options.B: A defined area on land or water (including buildings, installations and equipment) intended to be used either wholly or in part for arrival, departure and surface movement of aircraft
options.C: A facility that provides air traffic control services for arriving and departing aircraft
options.D: An area with runways and taxiways only, excluding buildings
options.E: A defined area on land used exclusively for commercial air transport
options.F: A water area designed for seaplane operations only

explanation: Section 1.1 Definitions: 'Aerodrome. A defined area on land or water (including any buildings, installations and equipment) intended to be used either wholly or in part for the arrival, departure and surface movement of aircraft.'

question_text: Which of the following correctly describes 'Aerodrome traffic density - Light'?

options.A: Number of movements in mean busy hour greater than 15 per runway
options.B: Number of movements in mean busy hour not greater than 15 per runway or typically less than 20 total aerodrome movements
options.C: Number of movements in mean busy hour of the order of 16 to 25 per runway
options.D: Number of movements in mean busy hour of the order of 26 or more per runway
options.E: Number of movements in mean busy hour greater than 35 total aerodrome movements
options.F: Number of movements in mean busy hour between 20 and 35 total aerodrome movements

explanation: Section 1.1 Definitions: 'Light. Where the number of movements in the mean busy hour is not greater than 15 per runway or typically less than 20 total aerodrome movements.'

question_text: What is the definition of 'Aeroplane reference field length'?

options.A: The actual length of runway available for take-off at a specific aerodrome
options.B: The minimum field length required for take-off at maximum certificated take-off mass at sea level, standard atmospheric conditions, still air and zero runway slope
options.C: The distance required to accelerate to a decision speed and stop
options.D: The length of runway needed for landing under wet conditions
options.E: The balanced field length for all aircraft types
options.F: The take-off distance available including clearway

explanation: Section 1.1 Definitions: 'Aeroplane reference field length. The minimum field length required for take-off at maximum certificated take-off mass at sea level, standard atmospheric conditions, still air and zero runway slope, as shown in the appropriate aeroplane flight manual...'

question_text: True or False: The Aircraft Classification Number (ACN) is always calculated using the forwardmost centre of gravity position.
explanation: Section 1.1 Definitions: 'Normally the aftmost CG position appropriate to the maximum gross apron (ramp) mass is used to calculate the ACN. In exceptional cases the forwardmost CG position may result in the nose gear loading being more critical.'

question_text: What is a 'Barrette' as defined in Annex 14 Volume I?

options.B: Three or more aeronautical ground lights closely spaced in a transverse line so that from a distance they appear as a short bar of light
options.C: A flashing light used on approach paths
options.D: A type of runway edge light
options.E: A marker board used for unserviceability
options.F: A group of lights arranged in a circle

explanation: Section 1.1 Definitions: 'Barrette. Three or more aeronautical ground lights closely spaced in a transverse line so that from a distance they appear as a short bar of light.'

question_text: Which of the following is NOT one of the declared distances?

options.F: All of the above are declared distances

explanation: Section 1.1 Definitions lists: TORA, TODA, ASDA, LDA. ESDA is not a declared distance in Annex 14.

question_text: What is the definition of 'Hot spot'?

options.A: A location where aircraft frequently experience turbulence
options.B: A location on an aerodrome movement area with a history or potential risk of collision or runway incursion
options.C: An area where de-icing is required
options.D: A section of runway with low friction characteristics
options.E: A taxiway intersection with high traffic density
options.F: A parking stand prone to foreign object debris

explanation: Section 1.1 Definitions: 'Hot spot. A location on an aerodrome movement area with a history or potential risk of collision or runway incursion, and where heightened attention by pilots/drivers is necessary.'

question_text: True or False: The specifications in Annex 14 Volume I apply to stolports.
explanation: Section 1.2.2: 'The specifications in this volume shall apply, where appropriate, to heliports but shall not apply to stolports.'

question_text: Which horizontal reference system shall be used for reported aeronautical geographical coordinates according to Annex 14 Volume I?
explanation: Section 1.3.1: 'World Geodetic System — 1984 (WGS-84) shall be used as the horizontal (geodetic) reference system. Reported aeronautical geographical coordinates (indicating latitude and longitude) shall be expressed in terms of the WGS-84 geodetic reference datum.'

question_text: What vertical reference system shall be used according to Annex 14 Volume I?

options.B: Mean sea level (MSL) datum, which gives the relationship of gravity-related height to the geoid
options.C: Orthometric height datum based on WGS-84 ellipsoid

explanation: Section 1.3.2: 'Mean sea level (MSL) datum, which gives the relationship of gravity-related height (elevation) to a surface known as the geoid, shall be used as the vertical reference system.'

question_text: Which temporal reference system(s) shall be used according to Annex 14 Volume I?
explanation: Section 1.3.3.1: 'The Gregorian calendar and Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) shall be used as the temporal reference system.'

question_text: True or False: States shall certify aerodromes used for international operations in accordance with the specifications in Annex 14 and other relevant ICAO specifications through an appropriate regulatory framework.
explanation: Section 1.4.1: 'States shall certify aerodromes used for international operations in accordance with the specifications contained in this Annex as well as other relevant ICAO specifications through an appropriate regulatory framework.'

question_text: As part of the certification process, what document must be submitted by the applicant for approval prior to granting an aerodrome certificate?

options.B: Aerodrome manual including safety management system

explanation: Section 1.4.4: 'States shall ensure that an aerodrome manual which will include all pertinent information on the aerodrome site, facilities, services, equipment, operating procedures, organization and management including a safety management system, is submitted by the applicant for approval/acceptance prior to granting the aerodrome certificate.'

question_text: According to the Introductory Note of section 1.5, what does a master plan convey?

options.A: A confirmed implementation programme for aerodrome development
options.B: The ultimate development in a phased manner and reports the data and logic upon which the plan is based
options.C: Only the economic feasibility of aerodrome expansion
options.D: The environmental impact of aerodrome operations
options.E: The air traffic control procedures for the aerodrome
options.F: The security measures in place at the aerodrome

explanation: Section 1.5 Introductory Note: 'A master plan for the long-term development of an aerodrome conveys the ultimate development in a phased manner and reports the data and logic upon which the plan is based.'

question_text: What is the purpose of the Aerodrome Reference Code?

options.A: To determine runway length and pavement strength requirements
options.B: To provide a simple method for interrelating numerous specifications concerning aerodrome characteristics so as to provide facilities suitable for the aeroplanes intended to operate
options.C: To classify aeroplanes by their take-off weight
options.D: To assign a unique identifier to each aerodrome
options.E: To specify the type of approach lighting required
options.F: To determine the required rescue and firefighting category

explanation: Section 1.6 Introductory Note: 'The intent of the reference code is to provide a simple method for interrelating the numerous specifications concerning the characteristics of aerodromes so as to provide a series of aerodrome facilities that are suitable for the aeroplanes that are intended to operate at the aerodrome.'

question_text: According to Table 1-1, what is the code number for an aeroplane with a reference field length of 1 200 m up to but not including 1 800 m?
explanation: Table 1-1: Code element 1 - Code number 3 corresponds to '1 200 m up to but not including 1 800 m'.

question_text: According to Table 1-1, which code letter corresponds to a wingspan of 24 m up to but not including 36 m?
explanation: Table 1-1: Code element 2 - Code letter C corresponds to '24 m up to but not including 36 m'.

question_text: True or False: The code number for element 1 is determined by selecting the code number corresponding to the highest value of the aeroplane reference field lengths of the aeroplanes for which the runway is intended.
explanation: Section 1.6.3: 'The code number for element 1 shall be determined from Table 1-1 selecting the code number corresponding to the highest value of the aeroplane reference field lengths of the aeroplanes for which the runway is intended.'

question_text: When an aerodrome accommodates an aeroplane that exceeds the certificated characteristics of the aerodrome, what must be done?

options.A: The aeroplane is prohibited from using the aerodrome
options.B: The compatibility between the operation of the aeroplane and aerodrome infrastructure shall be assessed and appropriate measures developed to maintain an acceptable level of safety
options.C: The aerodrome must be immediately upgraded to meet the aeroplane's characteristics
options.D: No action is required if the aeroplane operates only during daytime
options.E: The aeroplane operator must provide a waiver
options.F: The aerodrome must close the runway for that aeroplane

explanation: Section 1.7.1: 'When the aerodrome accommodates an aeroplane that exceeds the certificated characteristics of the aerodrome, the compatibility between the operation of the aeroplane and aerodrome infrastructure and operations shall be assessed and appropriate measures developed and implemented in order to maintain an acceptable level of safety during operations.'

question_text: What is the recommended location for an aerodrome reference point?

options.A: At the geometric centre of the runway
options.B: Near the initial or planned geometric centre of the aerodrome
options.C: At the threshold of the primary runway
options.E: At the highest point of the landing area
options.F: At the entrance to the apron

explanation: Section 2.2.2: 'The aerodrome reference point shall be located near the initial or planned geometric centre of the aerodrome and shall normally remain where first established.'

question_text: To what accuracy shall the aerodrome elevation and geoid undulation be measured for an aerodrome used by international civil aviation?
explanation: Section 2.3.1: 'The aerodrome elevation and geoid undulation at the aerodrome elevation position shall be measured to the accuracy of one-half metre or foot and reported to the aeronautical information services authority.'

question_text: For a precision approach runway, to what accuracy shall the elevation and geoid undulation of the threshold, runway end, and highest elevation of the touchdown zone be measured?
explanation: Section 2.3.3: 'For precision approach runway, the elevation and geoid undulation of the threshold, the elevation of the runway end and the highest elevation of the touchdown zone shall be measured to the accuracy of one-quarter metre or foot and reported to the aeronautical information services authority.'

question_text: How is the aerodrome reference temperature defined (recommended practice)?

options.A: The monthly mean of the daily maximum temperatures for the hottest month of the year, averaged over a period of years
options.B: The highest temperature ever recorded at the aerodrome
options.C: The average of daily minimum temperatures for the coldest month
options.D: The temperature at which ice formation occurs
options.E: The mean temperature of the busiest month
options.F: The temperature at the time of aircraft operations

explanation: Section 2.4.2: 'The aerodrome reference temperature should be the monthly mean of the daily maximum temperatures for the hottest month of the year (the hottest month being that which has the highest monthly mean temperature). This temperature should be averaged over a period of years.'

question_text: To what precision shall the true bearing of a runway be measured?
explanation: Section 2.5.1 a): 'runway — true bearing to one-hundredth of a degree, designation number, length, width, displaced threshold location to the nearest metre or foot, slope, surface type, type of runway...'

question_text: True or False: The geographical coordinates of each threshold shall be measured and reported in degrees, minutes, seconds and tenths of seconds.
explanation: Section 2.5.2: 'The geographical coordinates of each threshold shall be measured and reported to the aeronautical information services authority in degrees, minutes, seconds and hundredths of seconds.' (not tenths)

question_text: For a pavement intended for aircraft with apron mass greater than 5700 kg, which method shall be used to report bearing strength (applicable until 27 November 2024)?
explanation: Section 2.6.2 (Applicable until 27 November 2024): 'The bearing strength of a pavement intended for aircraft of apron (ramp) mass greater than 5700 kg shall be made available using the aircraft classification number-pavement classification number (ACN-PCN) method...'

question_text: In the ACN-PCN method, what does the code 'R' stand for?
explanation: Section 2.6.6 a): Pavement type for ACN-PCN determination: Code 'R' for Rigid pavement, 'F' for Flexible pavement.

question_text: What is the maximum allowable tire pressure category code for unlimited pressure?
explanation: Section 2.6.6 c): Maximum allowable tire pressure category: Code W for Unlimited (no pressure limit), X for High (1.75 MPa), Y for Medium (1.25 MPa), Z for Low (0.50 MPa).

question_text: For a pavement intended for aircraft of apron mass equal to or less than 5700 kg, what information shall be reported?

options.B: Maximum allowable aircraft mass and maximum allowable tire pressure

explanation: Section 2.6.8: 'The bearing strength of a pavement intended for aircraft of apron (ramp) mass equal to or less than 5700 kg shall be made available by reporting the following information: a) maximum allowable aircraft mass; and b) maximum allowable tire pressure.'

question_text: What is the recommended location for a pre-flight altimeter check location?

options.E: In the air traffic control tower

explanation: Section 2.7.2: 'A pre-flight check location should be located on an apron.'

question_text: Which declared distances must be calculated for a runway intended for use by international commercial air transport?
explanation: Section 2.8: 'The following distances shall be calculated to the nearest metre or foot for a runway intended for use by international commercial air transport: a) take-off run available; b) take-off distance available; c) accelerate-stop distance available; and d) landing distance available.'

question_text: For an aerodrome with reference code number 3 or 4, how many inspections of the movement area are required each day?
explanation: Section 2.9.3 a): 'for the movement area, at least once where the aerodrome reference code number is 1 or 2 and at least twice where the aerodrome reference code number is 3 or 4'

question_text: True or False: The runway surface condition 'Wet ice' includes ice with water on top of it or ice that is melting.
explanation: Section 2.9.5 lists 'WET ICE' as a runway surface condition descriptor, and the definition earlier in section 1.1 e) vii) states: 'Wet ice. Ice with water on top of it or ice that is melting.'

question_text: What is the recommendation regarding friction measurements on runway surface conditions with contaminants other than compacted snow and ice?

options.A: They should be reported as accurate
options.C: They should be reported only if above a threshold
options.D: They are mandatory for all contaminants
options.E: They should be used to determine RWYCC
options.F: They should be reported after calibration

explanation: Section 2.9.8: 'Friction measurements made on runway surface conditions with contaminants other than compacted snow and ice should not be reported.'

question_text: What information concerning a visual approach slope indicator system must be made available?

options.B: Runway designation, type of system, side of runway, displacement angle, nominal approach slope angle, and minimum eye height over threshold
options.C: Only the nominal approach slope angle
options.F: Only the colour of the lights

explanation: Section 2.12 lists: a) associated runway designation number; b) type of system; c) angle of displacement and direction; d) nominal approach slope angle(s); e) minimum eye height(s) over the threshold.

question_text: What is the recommended usability factor for runways at an aerodrome?
explanation: Section 3.1.1: 'The number and orientation of runways at an aerodrome should be such that the usability factor of the aerodrome is not less than 95 per cent for the aeroplanes that the aerodrome is intended to serve.'

question_text: For aeroplanes whose reference field length is 1500 m or more, what maximum permissible crosswind component should be assumed for usability factor calculation (under normal circumstances)?
explanation: Section 3.1.3: '37 km/h (20 kt) in the case of aeroplanes whose reference field length is 1500 m or over'

question_text: When a threshold is displaced due to an unserviceable runway condition, what length of cleared and graded area should be available between the unserviceable area and the displaced threshold?
explanation: Section 3.1.6: 'Where this displacement is due to an unserviceable runway condition, a cleared and graded area of at least 60 m in length should be available between the unserviceable area and the displaced threshold.'

question_text: For a runway width of 45 m, how many stripes are required in the threshold marking according to the standard pattern?
explanation: Section 5.2.4.5 table: Runway width 45 m corresponds to 12 stripes.

question_text: What colour are taxiway markings?
explanation: Section 5.2.1.5: 'Taxiway markings, runway turn pad markings and aircraft stand markings shall be yellow.'

question_text: What is the colour of runway markings?
explanation: Section 5.2.1.4: 'Runway markings shall be white.'

question_text: True or False: The aiming point marking on a paved instrument runway with code number 2 shall commence no closer to the threshold than 250 m.
explanation: Table 5-1: For landing distance available 800 m up to but not including 1 200 m, distance from threshold to beginning of marking is 250 m. Code number 2 runway typically has landing distance in that range.

question_text: What is the purpose of a mandatory instruction sign?

options.A: To provide directional information to pilots
options.B: To identify a location beyond which an aircraft taxiing or vehicle shall not proceed unless authorized by the aerodrome control tower
options.C: To indicate the distance to the runway end

explanation: Section 5.4.2.1: 'A mandatory instruction sign shall be provided to identify a location beyond which an aircraft taxiing or vehicle shall not proceed unless authorized by the aerodrome control tower.'

question_text: What colour is the background of a mandatory instruction sign?
explanation: Section 5.4.2.12: 'A mandatory instruction sign shall consist of an inscription in white on a red background.'

question_text: What is the flash frequency for an aerodrome beacon?
explanation: Section 5.3.3.6: 'The frequency of total flashes shall be from 20 to 30 per minute.'

question_text: What colour do identification beacons show at a land aerodrome?
explanation: Section 5.3.3.12: 'An identification beacon shall show flashing-green at a land aerodrome and flashing-yellow at a water aerodrome.'

question_text: For a precision approach category I lighting system, what is the recommended length of the centre line row of lights?
explanation: Section 5.3.4.10: 'A precision approach category I lighting system shall consist of a row of lights on the extended centre line of the runway extending, wherever possible, over a distance of 900 m from the runway threshold...'

question_text: What is the colour of runway threshold lights in the direction of approach?
explanation: Section 5.3.10.9: 'Runway threshold and wing bar lights shall be fixed unidirectional lights showing green in the direction of approach to the runway.'

question_text: True or False: Runway centre line lights on a precision approach runway category II or III must show red from the threshold to the runway end.
explanation: Section 5.3.12.7: 'Runway centre line lights shall be fixed lights showing variable white from the threshold to the point 900 m from the runway end; alternate red and variable white from 900 m to 300 m from the runway end; and red from 300 m to the runway end.'

question_text: What is the colour of taxiway edge lights?
explanation: Section 5.3.18.7: 'Taxiway edge lights shall be fixed lights showing blue.'

question_text: When must a stop bar be provided at a runway-holding position?

options.B: When the runway is intended to be used in runway visual range conditions less than 550 m, with exceptions
options.F: When the runway is used for take-off only

explanation: Section 5.3.20.1: 'A stop bar shall be provided at every runway-holding position serving a runway when it is intended that the runway will be used in runway visual range conditions less than a value of 550 m except where: a) appropriate aids and procedures are available to assist in preventing inadvertent incursions ... or b) operational procedures exist to limit ...'

question_text: True or False: Runway guard lights Configuration A consist of three pairs of yellow lights.
explanation: Section 5.3.23.7: 'Runway guard lights, Configuration A, shall consist of two pairs of yellow lights.'

question_text: What is the minimum height of an obstacle that requires marking and lighting according to Annex 14?

options.B: Only those that penetrate obstacle limitation surfaces

explanation: Section 6.1.1 specifies various conditions for marking and lighting obstacles, primarily those that extend above obstacle limitation surfaces (approach, transitional, horizontal, etc.). There is no universal minimum height; it depends on penetration of surfaces.
sub_chapter: 6.1 Objects to be marked and/or lighted

question_text: What type of obstacle light is recommended for follow-me vehicles?
explanation: Section 6.2.2.7: 'Low-intensity obstacle lights, Type D, shall be displayed on follow-me vehicles.'

question_text: When a runway or taxiway is permanently closed, what must be done with the markings?

options.A: They must be covered with temporary material
options.C: They must be painted over with yellow
options.D: They must be left as is
options.E: They must be converted to closed markings
options.F: They must be removed by grinding

explanation: Section 7.1.5: 'When a runway or taxiway or portion thereof is permanently closed, all normal runway and taxiway markings shall be obliterated.'

question_text: For a precision approach runway category I, what is the maximum switch-over time required for the approach lighting system?
explanation: Table 8-1: For precision approach category I, the approach lighting system maximum switch-over time is 15 seconds.

question_text: How often must a full-scale aerodrome emergency exercise be conducted?

options.B: At intervals not exceeding two years
options.C: At intervals not exceeding three years

explanation: Section 9.1.13 a): 'a full-scale aerodrome emergency exercise at intervals not exceeding two years and partial emergency exercises in the intervening year...'

question_text: What is the operational objective for rescue and firefighting response time to any point of each operational runway in optimum visibility and surface conditions?
explanation: Section 9.2.26: 'The operational objective of the rescue and firefighting service shall be to achieve a response time not exceeding three minutes to any point of each operational runway, in optimum visibility and surface conditions.'

question_text: True or False: A fence or other suitable barrier must be provided on an aerodrome only to prevent entrance of animals, not unauthorized persons.
explanation: Section 9.10.1 requires a fence to prevent entrance of animals large enough to be a hazard. Section 9.10.2 requires a fence to deter access of unauthorized persons onto a non-public area. So both purposes.

question_text: What is the minimum average texture depth recommended for a new runway surface?
explanation: Section 3.1.26: 'The average surface texture depth of a new surface should be not less than 1.0 mm.'

question_text: According to Chapter 3, a runway turn pad shall be provided at the end of a runway not served by a taxiway and where the code letter is D, E or F. What is the purpose?

options.A: To allow for parking of aircraft
options.B: To facilitate a 180-degree turn of aeroplanes
options.D: To serve as a holding bay

explanation: Section 3.3.1: 'Where the end of a runway is not served by a taxiway or a taxiway turnaround and where the code letter is D, E or F, a runway turn pad shall be provided to facilitate a 180-degree turn of aeroplanes.'

question_text: What is the recommended maximum longitudinal slope for a runway where the code number is 3 or 4?
explanation: Section 3.1.13: 'The slope computed by dividing the difference between the maximum and minimum elevation along the runway centre line by the runway length should not exceed: 1 per cent where the code number is 3 or 4; and 2 per cent where the code number is 1 or 2.'

question_text: For a precision approach runway category II or III with code number 4, what is the maximum longitudinal slope allowed for the first and last quarter of the runway length?
explanation: Section 3.1.14: 'Along no portion of a runway should the longitudinal slope exceed: — 1.25 per cent where the code number is 4, except that for the first and last quarter of the length of the runway the longitudinal slope should not exceed 0.8 per cent;'

question_text: For a runway with code number 4, what is the maximum rate of change of slope (per 30 m) allowed for transition from one slope to another?
explanation: Section 3.1.16: 'The transition from one slope to another should be accomplished by a curved surface with a rate of change not exceeding: — 0.1 per cent per 30 m (minimum radius of curvature of 30 000 m) where the code number is 4;'

question_text: For a runway where the code letter is C, D, E or F, what is the minimum unobstructed line of sight requirement?

options.A: From any point 1.5 m above runway to all other points 1.5 m above within half runway length
options.B: From any point 2 m above runway to all other points 2 m above within half runway length
options.C: From any point 3 m above runway to all other points 3 m above within half runway length
options.D: From any point 4 m above runway to all other points 4 m above within half runway length
options.E: From any point 5 m above runway to all other points 5 m above within half runway length
options.F: From any point 0.5 m above runway to all other points 0.5 m above within full runway length

explanation: Section 3.1.17: 'Where slope changes cannot be avoided, they should be such that there will be an unobstructed line of sight from: — any point 3 m above a runway to all other points 3 m above the runway within a distance of at least half the length of the runway where the code letter is C, D, E or F;'

question_text: What is the recommended transverse slope for a runway where the code letter is C, D, E or F?
explanation: Section 3.1.19: 'The transverse slope should ideally be: 1.5 per cent where the code letter is C, D, E or F and 2 per cent where the code letter is A or B'

question_text: True or False: Runway shoulders are required for runways where the code letter is D, E or F.
explanation: Section 3.2.1: 'Runway shoulders should be provided for a runway where the code letter is D, E or F.'

question_text: For a runway with code letter F and four (or more) engined aeroplanes, what is the recommended overall width of the runway and its shoulders?
explanation: Section 3.2.2: '...the overall width of the runway and its shoulders is not less than: 60 m where the code letter is F with two- or three- engined aeroplanes; and 75 m where the code letter is F with four (or more)- engined aeroplanes.'

question_text: For a strip including a precision approach runway with code number 3 or 4, what is the recommended lateral extent on each side of the centre line?
explanation: Section 3.4.3: 'A strip including a precision approach runway shall, wherever practicable, extend laterally to a distance of at least: 140 m where the code number is 3 or 4; and 70 m where the code number is 1 or 2; on each side of the centre line...'

question_text: A runway end safety area (RESA) is required at each end of a runway strip for which conditions?

options.A: Code number 1 or 2 and non-instrument runway only
options.B: Code number 3 or 4, and also for code number 1 or 2 if instrument runway
options.C: All runways regardless of code number
options.F: Only for runways used at night

explanation: Section 3.5.1: 'A runway end safety area shall be provided at each end of a runway strip where: the code number is 3 or 4; and the code number is 1 or 2 and the runway is an instrument one.'

question_text: What is the minimum distance a RESA must extend from the end of the runway strip for code number 3 or 4?
explanation: Section 3.5.3: 'A runway end safety area shall extend from the end of a runway strip to a distance of at least 90 m where: the code number is 3 or 4; and the code number is 1 or 2 and the runway is an instrument one.'

question_text: What is the recommended maximum length of a clearway relative to the take-off run available?

options.A: Not exceed half the length of TORA
options.B: Not exceed one quarter of TORA

explanation: Section 3.6.2: 'The length of a clearway should not exceed half the length of the take-off run available.'

question_text: A stopway must have the same width as which feature?

options.B: The runway with which it is associated

explanation: Section 3.7.1: 'A stopway shall have the same width as the runway with which it is associated.'

question_text: What is the recommended length of a radio altimeter operating area before the threshold of a precision approach runway?
explanation: Section 3.8.2: 'A radio altimeter operating area should extend before the threshold for a distance of at least 300 m.'

question_text: For a taxiway intended for use by aeroplanes with OMGWS 9 m up to but not including 15 m, what is the minimum clearance distance between the outer main wheel and the edge of the taxiway on straight portions?
explanation: Section 3.9.3 table: For OMGWS 9 m up to but not including 15 m, clearance on straight portions is 4 m.

question_text: What is the recommended width of a straight taxiway for aeroplanes with OMGWS 9 m up to but not including 15 m?
explanation: Section 3.9.4 table: For OMGWS 9 m up to but not including 15 m, taxiway width is 23 m.

question_text: True or False: For a code letter F, the separation distance between taxiway centre line and runway centre line for instrument runways code number 4 is 180 m.
explanation: Table 3-1: For code letter F, distance between taxiway centre line and runway centre line (instrument runways code number 4) is 180 m.

question_text: What is the maximum longitudinal slope recommended for a taxiway where the code letter is C, D, E or F?
explanation: Section 3.9.8: 'The longitudinal slope of a taxiway should not exceed: 1.5 per cent where the code letter is C, D, E or F; and 3 per cent where the code letter is A or B.'

question_text: For a rapid exit taxiway with code number 3 or 4, what is the recommended minimum radius of turn-off curve to enable exit speeds under wet conditions of 93 km/h?
explanation: Section 3.9.15: 'A rapid exit taxiway should be designed with a radius of turn-off curve of at least: 550 m where the code number is 3 or 4; and 275 m where the code number is 1 or 2; to enable exit speeds under wet conditions of: 93 km/h where the code number is 3 or 4; and 65 km/h where the code number is 1 or 2.'

question_text: What is the recommended intersection angle of a rapid exit taxiway with the runway?

options.A: Not greater than 45° nor less than 25°, preferably 30°
options.C: Not greater than 90° nor less than 60°

explanation: Section 3.9.18: 'The intersection angle of a rapid exit taxiway with the runway should not be greater than 45° nor less than 25° and preferably should be 30°.'

question_text: For a taxiway with code letter F, what is the recommended overall width (including shoulders) on straight portions?
explanation: Section 3.10.1: 'Straight portions of a taxiway where the code letter is C, D, E or F should be provided with shoulders... so that the overall width... is not less than: 44 m where the code letter is F; 38 m where the code letter is E; 34 m where the code letter is D; and 25 m where the code letter is C.'

question_text: A runway-holding position shall be established at which locations?

options.B: At taxiway/runway intersections and at runway/runway intersections when the former runway is part of a standard taxi-route
options.D: At all taxiway intersections regardless of runway
options.E: Only at the threshold of each runway
options.F: Only at the end of each taxiway

explanation: Section 3.12.2: 'A runway-holding position or positions shall be established: a) on the taxiway, at the intersection of a taxiway and a runway; and b) at an intersection of a runway with another runway when the former runway is part of a standard taxi-route.'

question_text: What is the recommended minimum clearance for an aircraft stand with code letter D?
explanation: Section 3.13.6 table: Code letter clearance: D = 7.5 m, E = 7.5 m, F = 7.5 m.

question_text: Where should an isolated aircraft parking position be located?

options.A: As close as possible to the terminal
options.B: At the maximum distance practicable, never less than 100 m from other parking positions, buildings or public areas
options.E: In the centre of the apron

explanation: Section 3.14.2: 'The isolated aircraft parking position should be located at the maximum distance practicable and in any case never less than 100 m from other parking positions, buildings or public areas, etc.'

question_text: What is the recommended maximum slope on an aircraft stand?
explanation: Section 3.13.5: 'On an aircraft stand the maximum slope should not exceed 1 per cent.'

question_text: According to Chapter 4, what is the slope of the conical surface?
explanation: Table 4-1: Conical slope is 5% for all runway classifications and code numbers.

question_text: What is the height of the inner horizontal surface for an aerodrome with code number 3 or 4 (precision approach)?
explanation: Table 4-1: For precision approach runways (categories I, II, III) with code numbers 1,2,3,4, the inner horizontal height is 45 m.

question_text: For a precision approach runway category I, what is the slope of the inner approach surface?
explanation: Table 4-1: For precision approach (categories I, II, III) with code numbers 1,2,3,4, the inner approach slope is 2% (for code numbers 3,4) or 2.5% (for code numbers 1,2). For category I specifically, code numbers 3 and 4 have inner approach slope 2%.

question_text: For a non-instrument runway with code number 1, what is the length of the inner edge of the approach surface?
explanation: Table 4-1: For non-instrument runways code number 1, length of inner edge is 60 m.

question_text: What is the slope of the take-off climb surface for code number 3 or 4?
explanation: Table 4-2: For runways meant for take-off, code number 3 or 4, slope is 2%.

question_text: True or False: For a precision approach runway category II or III, fixed objects are permitted above the inner approach surface only if they are frangible and required for navigation.
explanation: Section 4.2.18: 'Fixed objects shall not be permitted above the inner approach surface, the inner transitional surface or the balked landing surface, except for frangible objects which because of their function must be located on the strip.'

question_text: What is the recommended height above ground elevation for objects to be regarded as obstacles outside obstacle limitation surfaces?
explanation: Section 4.3.2: 'In areas beyond the limits of the obstacle limitation surfaces, at least those objects which extend to a height of 150 m or more above ground elevation should be regarded as obstacles, unless a special aeronautical study indicates that they do not constitute a hazard to aeroplanes.'

question_text: What is the minimum length of a wind direction indicator?
explanation: Section 5.1.1.3: 'The wind direction indicator should be in the form of a truncated cone made of fabric and should have a length of not less than 3.6 m...'

question_text: What colour is a landing 'T' indicator recommended to be?
explanation: Section 5.1.2.3: 'The colour of the landing 'T' shall be either white or orange, the choice being dependent on the colour that contrasts best with the background...'

question_text: What is the minimum intensity of a signalling lamp intended for daytime use?
explanation: Section 5.1.3.3: 'When the signalling lamp is intended for use in the daytime the intensity of the coloured light should be not less than 6000 cd.'

question_text: What are the minimum dimensions of a signal area?
explanation: Section 5.1.4.2: 'The signal area shall be an even horizontal surface at least 9 m square.'

question_text: For a precision approach category II or III runway, what is the minimum width of the runway centre line marking stripes?
explanation: Section 5.2.3.4: 'The width of the stripes shall be not less than: 0.90 m on precision approach category II and III runways;'

question_text: When a threshold is permanently displaced, what marking is required on the portion before the displaced threshold?
explanation: Section 5.2.4.9: 'Where a runway threshold is permanently displaced, arrows conforming to Figure 5-4 (B) shall be provided on the portion of the runway before the displaced threshold.'
image_description: Figure 5-4 (B) shows displaced threshold markings with arrows

question_text: What is the recommended lateral spacing between the inner sides of the aiming point marking stripes for a runway with landing distance available 2400 m or above?
explanation: Table 5-1: For landing distance available 2 400 m and above, lateral spacing between inner sides of stripes is 18–22.5 m.

question_text: How many pairs of touchdown zone markings are required when the landing distance available is 2400 m or more?
explanation: Section 5.2.6.3 table: For landing distance available 2 400 m or more, number of pairs of markings is 6.

question_text: What is the colour of taxiway centre line marking?
explanation: Section 5.2.1.5: 'Taxiway markings, runway turn pad markings and aircraft stand markings shall be yellow.' This includes centre line markings.

question_text: What is the minimum width of a taxiway centre line marking?
explanation: Section 5.2.8.10: 'A taxiway centre line marking shall be at least 15 cm in width and continuous in length...'

question_text: What is the pattern of a runway-holding position marking at an intersection of a taxiway and a non-instrument runway?

options.A: Pattern A (two solid lines, two dashed lines)

explanation: Section 5.2.10.2: 'At an intersection of a taxiway and a non-instrument, non-precision approach or take-off runway, the runway-holding position marking shall be as shown in Figure 5-6, pattern A.'

question_text: What is the colour of a VOR aerodrome checkpoint marking?

options.B: White (preferably) but should differ from taxiway marking colour

explanation: Section 5.2.12.6: 'A VOR aerodrome checkpoint marking should preferably be white in colour but should differ from the colour used for the taxiway markings.'

question_text: What is the recommended character height for mandatory instruction markings where code letter is C, D, E or F?
explanation: Section 5.2.16.9: 'The character height should be 4 m for inscriptions where the code letter is C, D, E or F, and 2 m where the code letter is A or B.'

question_text: What colour are information markings that replace or supplement a location sign?
explanation: Section 5.2.17.6 a): 'an inscription in yellow upon a black background, when it replaces or supplements a location sign;'

question_text: True or False: Elevated approach lights and their supporting structures must be frangible only up to 300 m from the threshold; beyond that, no frangibility requirement applies.
explanation: Section 5.3.1.4: 'Elevated approach lights and their supporting structures shall be frangible except that, in that portion of the approach lighting system beyond 300 m from the threshold: a) where the height of a supporting structure exceeds 12 m, the frangibility requirement shall apply to the top 12 m only; and b) where a supporting structure is surrounded by non-frangible objects, only that part of the structure that extends above the surrounding objects shall be frangible.' So frangibility does apply, but limited.

question_text: What is the maximum temperature that the interface between an installed inset light and an aircraft tire should not exceed during a 10-minute period of exposure?
explanation: Section 5.3.1.8: 'The temperature produced by conduction or radiation at the interface between an installed inset light and an aircraft tire should not exceed 160°C during a 10-minute period of exposure.'

question_text: What is the minimum effective intensity of an aerodrome beacon flash?
explanation: Section 5.3.3.7: 'the effective intensity of the flash shall be not less than 2000 cd.'

question_text: For a simple approach lighting system serving a non-precision approach runway, what is the recommended distance from threshold for the crossbar?
explanation: Section 5.3.4.2: 'A simple approach lighting system shall consist of a row of lights on the extended centre line of the runway extending, whenever possible, over a distance of not less than 420 m from the threshold with a row of lights forming a crossbar 18 m or 30 m in length at a distance of 300 m from the threshold.'

question_text: What is the longitudinal interval for centre line lights in a precision approach category I lighting system?
explanation: Section 5.3.4.12: 'The lights forming the centre line shall be placed at longitudinal intervals of 30 m with the innermost light located 30 m from the threshold.'

question_text: In a precision approach category II and III lighting system, what colour are the side row lights?
explanation: Section 5.3.4.36: 'The side row shall consist of barrettes showing red.'

question_text: According to Chapter 5, what is the standard system recommended to be discontinued as of 1 January 2020?
explanation: Section 5.3.5.4: 'As of 1 January 2020, the use of T-VASIS and AT-VASIS as standard visual approach slope indicator systems should be discontinued.'

question_text: In a PAPI system, how many light units does the wing bar consist of?
explanation: Section 5.3.5.24: 'The PAPI system shall consist of a wing bar of four sharp transition multi-lamp (or paired single lamp) units equally spaced.'

question_text: What is the recommended obstacle protection surface slope for a PAPI system on a non-instrument runway with code number 1?
explanation: Table 5-3: For non-instrument runways code number 1, PAPI slope is 'A - 0.57°' (where A is the approach slope angle).

question_text: True or False: Runway threshold identification lights should be flashing white with a flash frequency between 60 and 120 per minute.
explanation: Section 5.3.8.3: 'Runway threshold identification lights should be flashing white lights with a flash frequency between 60 and 120 per minute.'

question_text: What is the maximum spacing for runway edge lights on an instrument runway?
explanation: Section 5.3.9.6: 'The lights shall be uniformly spaced in rows at intervals of not more than 60 m for an instrument runway, and at intervals of not more than 100 m for a non-instrument runway.'

question_text: What colour do runway edge lights show in the approach direction at a displaced threshold?
explanation: Section 5.3.9.7 a): 'in the case of a displaced threshold, the lights between the beginning of the runway and the displaced threshold shall show red in the approach direction;'

question_text: For a precision approach runway category II or III, what is the maximum spacing between threshold lights?
explanation: Section 5.3.10.4 c): 'lights uniformly spaced between the rows of runway edge lights at intervals of not more than 3 m.'

question_text: What colour are runway threshold wing bar lights?
explanation: Section 5.3.10.9: 'Runway threshold and wing bar lights shall be fixed unidirectional lights showing green in the direction of approach to the runway.'

question_text: What is the colour of runway end lights in the direction of the runway?
explanation: Section 5.3.11.4: 'Runway end lights shall be fixed unidirectional lights showing red in the direction of the runway.'

question_text: What is the longitudinal spacing of runway centre line lights on a precision approach runway category II or III?
explanation: Section 5.3.12.5: 'The lights shall be located from the threshold to the end at longitudinal spacing of approximately 15 m. Where the serviceability level ... can be demonstrated and the runway is intended for use in runway visual range conditions of 350 m or greater, the longitudinal spacing may be approximately 30 m.'

question_text: What is the colour of runway centre line lights from 300 m to the runway end?
explanation: Section 5.3.12.7: 'runway centre line lights shall be fixed lights showing ... red from 300 m to the runway end.'

question_text: How far from the threshold do touchdown zone lights extend?
explanation: Section 5.3.13.2: 'Touchdown zone lights shall extend from the threshold for a longitudinal distance of 900 m, except that, on runways less than 1800 m in length, the system shall be shortened so that it does not extend beyond the midpoint of the runway.'

question_text: What is the colour of simple touchdown zone lights?
explanation: Section 5.3.14.4: 'Simple touchdown zone lights shall be fixed unidirectional lights showing variable white, aligned so as to be visible to the pilot of a landing aeroplane in the direction of approach to the runway.'

question_text: What colour are rapid exit taxiway indicator lights (RETILs)?
explanation: Section 5.3.15.5: 'Rapid exit taxiway indicator lights shall be fixed unidirectional yellow lights...'

question_text: What colour are stopway lights?
explanation: Section 5.3.16.3: 'Stopway lights shall be fixed unidirectional lights showing red in the direction of the runway.'

question_text: What is the colour of taxiway centre line lights on a taxiway other than an exit taxiway?
explanation: Section 5.3.17.6: 'taxiway centre line lights on a taxiway other than an exit taxiway ... shall be fixed lights showing green...'

question_text: On an exit taxiway, what is the colour of the first taxiway centre line light from the runway centre line?
explanation: Section 5.3.17.7: 'The first light in the exit centre line shall always show green, and the light nearest to the perimeter shall always show yellow.'

question_text: What colour are taxiway edge lights?
explanation: Section 5.3.18.7: 'Taxiway edge lights shall be fixed lights showing blue.'

question_text: What is the minimum intensity of taxiway edge lights from 0° to 6° vertical?
explanation: Section 5.3.18.8: 'The intensity of taxiway edge lights shall be at least 2 cd from 0° to 6° vertical, and 0.2 cd at any vertical angles between 6° and 75°.'

question_text: What colour are runway turn pad lights?
explanation: Section 5.3.19.6: 'Runway turn pad lights shall be unidirectional fixed lights showing green...'

question_text: What is the maximum spacing for lights in a stop bar across a taxiway?
explanation: Section 5.3.20.5: 'Stop bars shall consist of lights spaced at uniform intervals of no more than 3 m across the taxiway, showing red in the intended direction(s) of approach...'

question_text: When a stop bar is illuminated, what must happen to taxiway centre line lights beyond the stop bar?

options.C: They are extinguished for a distance of at least 90 m

explanation: Section 5.3.20.12 c): 'when a stop bar is illuminated, any taxiway centre line lights installed beyond the stop bar shall be extinguished for a distance of at least 90 m;'

question_text: What colour are intermediate holding position lights?
explanation: Section 5.3.21.4: 'Intermediate holding position lights shall consist of three fixed unidirectional lights showing yellow in the direction of approach to the intermediate holding position...'

question_text: What colour are de-icing/anti-icing facility exit lights?
explanation: Section 5.3.22.3: 'De-icing/anti-icing facility exit lights shall consist of in-pavement fixed unidirectional lights spaced at intervals of 6 m showing yellow...'

question_text: True or False: Runway guard lights Configuration A consist of two pairs of yellow lights.
explanation: Section 5.3.23.7: 'Runway guard lights, Configuration A, shall consist of two pairs of yellow lights.'

question_text: What is the recommended average horizontal illuminance on an aircraft stand?
explanation: Section 5.3.24.4: 'The average illuminance should be at least the following: Aircraft stand: horizontal illuminance 20 lux with a uniformity ratio (average to minimum) of not more than 4 to 1;'

question_text: What two types of guidance must a visual docking guidance system provide?
explanation: Section 5.3.25.2: 'The system shall provide both azimuth and stopping guidance.'

question_text: In an advanced visual docking guidance system (A-VDGS), what colour word should be displayed when an immediate cessation of docking is required?
explanation: Section 5.3.26.16: 'The word 'stop' in red characters should be displayed when an immediate cessation of the docking manoeuvre is required.'

question_text: What colour are aircraft stand manoeuvring guidance lights (other than stop position)?
explanation: Section 5.3.27.3: 'Aircraft stand manoeuvring guidance lights, other than those indicating a stop position, shall be fixed yellow lights...'

question_text: What is the flash frequency for a flashing-red road-holding position light?
explanation: Section 5.3.28.7: 'The flash frequency of the flashing-red light shall be between 30 and 60 flashes per minute.'

question_text: Where should a no-entry bar be located?

options.A: At the threshold of a runway
options.B: Across a taxiway at the end of an exit only taxiway to prevent wrong-way entry
options.C: At the entrance to the apron
options.F: At the fuel station access road

explanation: Section 5.3.29.2: 'A no-entry bar should be located across the taxiway at the end of an exit only taxiway where it is desired to prevent traffic from entering the taxiway in the wrong direction.'

question_text: What colour are runway entrance lights (RELs) in a runway status lights system?
explanation: Section 5.3.30.4: 'RELs shall consist of a single line of fixed in pavement lights showing red in the direction of aircraft approaching the runway.'

question_text: What is the minimum character height for a mandatory instruction sign on a runway code number 3 or 4?
explanation: Appendix 4, section 1 table: For runway code number 3 or 4, mandatory instruction sign minimum character height is 400 mm.

question_text: What colour is the background of a direction sign?
explanation: Section 5.4.3.25: 'An information sign other than a location sign shall consist of an inscription in black on a yellow background.' Direction signs are information signs.

question_text: What is the recommended character height for an aerodrome identification sign?
explanation: Section 5.4.5.5: 'The characters should have a height of not less than 3 m.'

question_text: What is the colour of a taxiway centre line marker?
explanation: Section 5.5.6.5: 'A taxiway centre line marker shall be retroreflective green.'

question_text: What colour is a taxiway edge marker?
explanation: Section 5.5.5.3: 'A taxiway edge marker shall be retroreflective blue.'

question_text: True or False: Low-intensity obstacle lights, Type C, displayed on vehicles associated with emergency or security shall be flashing-yellow.
explanation: Section 6.2.2.6: 'Low-intensity obstacle lights, Type C, displayed on vehicles associated with emergency or security shall be flashing-blue and those displayed on other vehicles shall be flashing-yellow.'

question_text: What is the recommended peak intensity for medium-intensity obstacle lights, Type A, during daytime (background luminescence above 500 cd/m²)?
explanation: Table 6-1: Medium-intensity Type A, white flashing, peak intensity at given Background Luminescence: Day (Above 500 cd/m²) = 20 000 cd.

question_text: For a chimney or other structure of like function, where should the top obstacle lights be placed?

options.A: At the very top of the structure
options.B: Sufficiently below the top to minimize contamination by smoke
options.E: On the ground surrounding the structure

explanation: Section 6.2.3.11: 'In the case of chimney or other structure of like function, the top lights should be placed sufficiently below the top so as to minimize contamination by smoke, etc. (See Figure 6-2).'

question_text: What is the recommended spacing for low-intensity obstacle lights on an extensive object?
explanation: Section 6.2.3.15 a): 'where low-intensity lights are used, they shall be spaced at longitudinal intervals not exceeding 45 m;'

question_text: What is the recommended overall colour for wind turbine rotor blades, nacelle, and upper 2/3 of the supporting mast?
explanation: Section 6.2.4.2: 'The rotor blades, nacelle and upper 2/3 of the supporting mast of wind turbine should be painted white, unless otherwise indicated by an aeronautical study.'

question_text: What is the recommended shape and minimum diameter for a marker displayed on an overhead wire?

options.A: Spherical, diameter not less than 30 cm
options.B: Spherical, diameter not less than 60 cm

explanation: Section 6.2.5.4: 'A marker displayed on an overhead wire, cable, etc., should be spherical and have a diameter of not less than 60 cm.'

question_text: What is the pattern of a closed marking on a runway?

options.B: A white X with arms at 45° to the centre line (Figure 7-1 illustration a)
options.D: A solid white line across the runway

explanation: Section 7.1.4: 'The closed marking shall be of the form and proportions as detailed in Figure 7-1, illustration a), when displayed on a runway...' The figure shows a white X with arms at 45° to the centre line.
image_description: Figure 7-1 illustration a) shows a white X marking for closed runway

question_text: What is the recommended colour for a pre-threshold chevron marking?
explanation: Section 7.3.3: 'A chevron marking should be of conspicuous colour and contrast with the colour used for the runway markings; it should preferably be yellow.'

question_text: What colour are unserviceability lights?
explanation: Section 7.4.4: 'An unserviceability light shall consist of a red fixed light.'

question_text: For a precision approach runway category II/III, what is the maximum switch-over time for the inner 300 m of the approach lighting system?
explanation: Table 8-1: For precision approach category II/III, inner 300 m of the approach lighting system maximum switch-over time is 1 second.

question_text: For a runway meant for take-off in runway visual range conditions less than 800 m, what is the maximum switch-over time for runway centre line lights?
explanation: Table 8-1: For runway meant for take-off in RVR < 800 m, runway centre line lights maximum switch-over time is 1 second.

question_text: True or False: The aerodrome emergency plan must be tested by a full-scale exercise at intervals not exceeding three years, with no partial exercises required.
explanation: Section 9.1.13: The plan shall be tested by conducting a full-scale exercise at intervals not exceeding two years and partial emergency exercises in the intervening year, or a series of modular tests concluding in a full-scale exercise at intervals not exceeding three years.

question_text: What is the response time objective for the first responding rescue and firefighting vehicle to any other part of the movement area (optimum conditions)?
explanation: Section 9.2.28: 'The operational objective of the rescue and firefighting service should be to achieve a response time not exceeding three minutes to any other part of the movement area, in optimum visibility and surface conditions.'

question_text: For an aerodrome category 6, what is the minimum number of rescue and firefighting vehicles recommended?
explanation: Section 9.2.40 table: Aerodrome category 6 requires 3 rescue and firefighting vehicles.

question_text: What is the principal extinguishing agent recommended for aerodromes in categories 1 to 3?

options.B: Foam meeting performance level B or C (preferably)

explanation: Section 9.2.9: '...except that the principal extinguishing agent for aerodromes in categories 1 to 3 should preferably meet a performance level B or C foam.'

question_text: According to Annex 14, what must be done to decrease the risk of wildlife strikes?

options.A: All wildlife on the aerodrome must be exterminated
options.B: Action shall be taken to adopt measures to minimize the likelihood of collisions between wildlife and aircraft
options.C: No action is required if strikes are rare
options.D: Only bird strikes need to be reported
options.E: A wildlife management programme is optional
options.F: Only mammals need to be controlled

explanation: Section 9.4.3: 'Action shall be taken to decrease the risk to aircraft operations by adopting measures to minimize the likelihood of collisions between wildlife and aircraft.'

question_text: True or False: An apron management service shall be provided with radiotelephony communications facilities.
explanation: Section 9.5.3: 'An apron management service shall be provided with radiotelephony communications facilities.'

question_text: During ground servicing of an aircraft, what fire extinguishing provision must be readily available?

options.B: Fire extinguishing equipment suitable for at least initial intervention in event of fuel fire
options.D: No equipment required if aircraft is small

explanation: Section 9.6.1: 'Fire extinguishing equipment suitable for at least initial intervention in the event of a fuel fire and personnel trained in its use shall be readily available during the ground servicing of an aircraft...'

question_text: Who shall authorize a vehicle to operate on the manoeuvring area?
explanation: Section 9.7.1 a): 'A vehicle shall be operated: a) on a manoeuvring area only as authorized by the aerodrome control tower;'

question_text: What is the requirement for equipment or installations that must be located within 75 m of the runway centre line for code number 3 or 4?

options.B: They shall be frangible and mounted as low as possible
options.D: They must be marked with flags
options.E: They must be enclosed by a fence

explanation: Section 9.9.2: 'Any equipment or installation required for air navigation or for aircraft safety purposes which must be located: a) on that portion of a runway strip within: 1) 75 m of the runway centre line where the code number is 3 or 4; ... shall be frangible and mounted as low as possible.'

question_text: According to Chapter 10, what is the objective of a maintenance programme at an aerodrome?

options.B: To maintain facilities in a condition which does not impair the safety, regularity or efficiency of air navigation
options.C: To extend the life of equipment

explanation: Section 10.1.1: 'A maintenance programme, including preventive maintenance where appropriate, shall be established at an aerodrome to maintain facilities in a condition which does not impair the safety, regularity or efficiency of air navigation.'

question_text: For a paved runway, how often should surface friction characteristics be measured for maintenance purposes?

options.B: Periodically, with sufficient frequency to determine the trend

explanation: Section 10.2.4: 'Runway surface friction characteristics for maintenance purposes shall be periodically measured with a continuous friction measuring device using self-wetting features and documented. The frequency of these measurements shall be sufficient to determine the trend of the surface friction characteristics of the runway.'

question_text: What is the maximum allowable longitudinal slope for a temporary ramp during runway pavement overlay, for overlays more than 5 cm in thickness?
explanation: Section 10.4.1: 'The longitudinal slope of the temporary ramp, measured with reference to the existing runway surface or previous overlay course, shall be: a) 0.5 to 1.0 per cent for overlays up to and including 5 cm in thickness; and b) not more than 0.5 per cent for overlays more than 5 cm in thickness.'

question_text: When is a light deemed unserviceable according to Chapter 10?

options.B: When the main beam average intensity is less than 50% of the value specified in Appendix 2
options.D: When it is out of alignment

explanation: Section 10.5.1: 'A light shall be deemed to be unserviceable when the main beam average intensity is less than 50 per cent of the value specified in the appropriate figure in Appendix 2.'

question_text: For a precision approach runway category II or III, what is the minimum serviceability objective for lights in the inner 450 m of the approach lighting system?
explanation: Section 10.5.7 a) 1): '95 per cent of the lights are serviceable in each of the following particular significant elements: 1) precision approach category II and III lighting system, the inner 450 m;'

question_text: For a precision approach runway category I, what is the minimum serviceability objective for the precision approach category I lighting system?
explanation: Section 10.5.10: 'The system of preventive maintenance employed for a precision approach runway category I shall have as its objective that, during any period of category I operations, all approach and runway lights are serviceable and that, in any event, at least 85 per cent of the lights are serviceable in each of the following: a) precision approach category I lighting system; ...'

question_text: According to Appendix 1, what is the yellow boundary for red aeronautical ground lights with filament-type sources, except for visual approach slope indicator systems?
explanation: Appendix 1, section 2.1.1 a): Red - Yellow boundary: y = 0.335, except for visual approach slope indicator systems (for which y = 0.320).

question_text: What is the recommended luminance factor for ordinary colour red for markings and externally illuminated signs and panels?
explanation: Appendix 1, section 3.2 a): Red - Luminance factor ß = 0.07 (min).

question_text: In Appendix 2, Figure A2-1, what is the minimum average intensity for approach centre line light (white light)?
explanation: Appendix 2, Figure A2-1: Minimum average intensity is 20 000 cd (labelled on the diagram).

question_text: True or False: According to Appendix 2, the average intensity of a light should never fall to a value less than 50% of the value shown in the figures.
explanation: Appendix 2, Collective notes to Figures A2-1 to A2-11 and A2-26, note 8: 'The importance of adequate maintenance cannot be overemphasized. The average intensity should never fall to a value less than 50 per cent of the value shown in the figures...'

question_text: What is the required average luminance for a mandatory instruction sign (red background) when operations are conducted in RVR less than 800 m?
explanation: Appendix 4, section 4 a): Where operations are conducted in runway visual range conditions less than a value of 800 m, average sign luminance shall be at least: Red 30 cd/m², Yellow 150 cd/m², White 300 cd/m².

question_text: In Appendix 4, what is the minimum character height for an information sign (other than runway exit/vacated) on runway code number 1 or 2?
explanation: Appendix 4, section 1 table: For runway code number 1 or 2, information sign (other signs) minimum character height is 200 mm.

question_text: According to Appendix 5, for a structure with height 150 m, what type of obstacle lighting is recommended on structures with a height of more than 150 m above ground level?
explanation: Appendix 5, note under Figure A5-1: 'High-intensity obstacle lighting is recommended on structures with a height of more than 150 m above ground level.'

question_text: In Attachment A, section 1.1.3, which factor is NOT listed under 'Topography of the aerodrome site'?

options.E: Possibility of installing visual and non-visual aids

explanation: Attachment A, section 1.1.3 lists a) compliance with OLS, b) land use, c) runway lengths, d) construction costs, e) possibility of installing aids. Proximity of other aerodromes is under 1.1.4 (Air traffic in vicinity).

question_text: What is the recommended maximum spacing between markers on overhead wires, for a marker diameter of 60 cm?
explanation: Attachment A, section 6.2.5.5 a): '30 m where the marker diameter is 60 cm'

question_text: In the context of runway surface evenness (Attachment A), what is the acceptable surface irregularity height for a length of 3 m?
explanation: Attachment A, section 5.3 table: For length of irregularity 3 m, acceptable surface irregularity height is 2.9 cm.

question_text: According to Attachment A, what is the mean texture depth classification for ESDU class E?
explanation: Attachment A, section 7.3.9 table: Classification E texture depths: 1.01 – 2.54 mm.

question_text: True or False: The obstacle protection surface for a visual approach slope indicator system has a total length of 15 000 m for instrument runways code number 3 or 4.
explanation: Table 5-3: For instrument runways code number 3 or 4, total length is 15 000 m (for PAPI and T-VASIS/AT-VASIS where applicable).

question_text: According to Attachment B (Figure B-1), what is the slope of the conical surface?
explanation: Attachment B, Figure B-1 (Obstacle limitation surfaces) labels the conical surface with 'Slope 5%'.
image_description: Figure B-1 shows obstacle limitation surfaces with conical slope 5%

question_text: What is the requirement for the driver of a vehicle on the movement area regarding mandatory instructions conveyed by lights?

options.A: They may be ignored if no other traffic is present
options.B: The driver shall comply with all mandatory instructions conveyed by lights
options.C: Only red lights must be obeyed
options.D: Only flashing lights must be obeyed
options.E: Instructions from lights are advisory only
options.F: The driver may proceed if safe

explanation: Section 9.7.3: 'The driver of a vehicle on the movement area shall comply with all mandatory instructions conveyed by lights.'

question_text: What is the recommended maximum transverse slope for a graded portion of a taxiway strip where the code letter is C, D, E or F?
explanation: Section 3.11.5: 'the graded portion should not have an upward transverse slope exceeding: 2.5 per cent for strips where the code letter is C, D, E or F; and 3 per cent for strips of taxiways where the code letter is A or B'

question_text: For a de-icing/anti-icing pad, what is the recommended clear paved area all round the aeroplane for movement of de-icing vehicles?
explanation: Section 3.15.5: 'The size of a de-icing/anti-icing pad should be equal to the parking area required by the most demanding aeroplane in a given category with at least 3.8 m clear paved area all round the aeroplane for the movement of the de-icing/anti-icing vehicles.'

question_text: What is the maximum slope allowed for a de-icing/anti-icing pad transverse slope?
explanation: Section 3.15.7: 'The maximum longitudinal slope should be as little as practicable and the transverse slope should not exceed 1 per cent.'

question_text: For a precision approach runway category II or III, which obstacle limitation surfaces shall be established?

options.A: Only inner approach and inner transitional surfaces
options.B: Conical, inner horizontal, approach and inner approach, transitional, inner transitional, and balked landing surfaces

explanation: Section 4.2.15: 'The following obstacle limitation surfaces shall be established for a precision approach runway category II or III: conical surface; inner horizontal surface; approach surface and inner approach surface; transitional surfaces; inner transitional surfaces; and balked landing surface.'
