question_text: What is the minimum response time objective for rescue and firefighting vehicles required to deliver the amounts of extinguishing agents specified in Table 9-2 (other than the first responding vehicles)?

options.A: No more than 2 minutes from initial call
options.B: No more than 3 minutes from initial call
options.C: No more than 4 minutes from initial call
options.D: No more than 5 minutes from initial call
options.E: No more than 6 minutes from initial call
options.F: No more than 10 minutes from initial call

explanation: Section 9.2.30: 'Any vehicles, other than the first responding vehicle(s), required to deliver the amounts of extinguishing agents specified in Table 9-2 shall ensure continuous agent application and shall arrive no more than four minutes from the initial call.'

question_text: What is the requirement for protective clothing for rescue and firefighting personnel?

options.B: All responding rescue and firefighting personnel shall be provided with protective clothing and respiratory equipment
options.C: Only the vehicle driver needs protective clothing
options.D: Protective clothing is optional for category 1 and 2 aerodromes

explanation: Section 9.2.45: 'All responding rescue and firefighting personnel shall be provided with protective clothing and respiratory equipment to enable them to perform their duties in an effective manner.'

question_text: According to Chapter 9, what should a disabled aircraft removal plan be based on?

options.A: The characteristics of aircraft that may normally be expected to operate at the aerodrome
options.B: The largest aircraft in the world
options.C: Only the aircraft that have previously had accidents at the aerodrome

explanation: Section 9.3.2: 'The disabled aircraft removal plan should be based on the characteristics of the aircraft that may normally be expected to operate at the aerodrome, and include among other things: a) a list of equipment and personnel on, or in the vicinity of, the aerodrome which would be available for such purpose; and b) arrangements for the rapid receipt of aircraft recovery equipment kits available from other aerodromes.'

question_text: What must be provided to an aircraft stand to ensure recommended clearance distances?
explanation: Section 9.5.7: 'An aircraft stand shall be visually monitored to ensure that the recommended clearance distances are provided to an aircraft using the stand.'

question_text: True or False: During low visibility procedures, persons and vehicles operating on an apron may continue normal operations without restriction.
explanation: Section 9.5.4: 'Where low visibility procedures are in effect, persons and vehicles operating on an apron shall be restricted to the essential minimum.'

question_text: What is the purpose of a surface movement guidance and control system (SMGCS)?

options.B: To provide guidance and control for surface movement at an aerodrome

explanation: Section 9.8.1: 'A surface movement guidance and control system (SMGCS) shall be provided at an aerodrome.' The purpose is implied from the name and the subsequent design recommendations (9.8.2 to 9.8.5).

question_text: What is the requirement for a fence at an aerodrome regarding animals?

options.A: A fence shall be provided to prevent entrance of animals large enough to be a hazard to aircraft
options.B: A fence is only required for security purposes
options.C: A fence is optional for animal control
options.E: A fence is only required for large animals like deer
options.F: No fence is required for animals

explanation: Section 9.10.1: 'A fence or other suitable barrier shall be provided on an aerodrome to prevent the entrance to the movement area of animals large enough to be a hazard to aircraft.'

question_text: According to Chapter 10, what should be inspected regularly as part of a preventive and corrective maintenance programme?

options.B: The surfaces of all movement areas including pavements (runways, taxiways, aprons) and adjacent areas

explanation: Section 10.2.1: 'The surfaces of all movement areas including pavements (runways, taxiways and aprons) and adjacent areas shall be inspected and their conditions monitored regularly as part of an aerodrome preventive and corrective maintenance programme...'

question_text: What chemicals shall not be used for pavement de-icing according to Chapter 10?

options.B: Chemicals which may have harmful effects on aircraft or pavements, or toxic effects on the environment

explanation: Section 10.3.6: 'Chemicals which may have harmful effects on aircraft or pavements, or chemicals which may have toxic effects on the aerodrome environment, shall not be used.'

question_text: For a precision approach runway category II or III, what is the recommended frequency of measurement of lights (in-pavement lights)?
explanation: Section 10.5.6: 'The frequency of measurement of lights for a precision approach runway category II or III should be based on ... but, in any event, should not be less than twice a year for in-pavement lights and not less than once a year for other lights.'

question_text: True or False: For a precision approach runway category II or III, an unserviceable light may be permitted adjacent to another unserviceable light in a barrette or crossbar.
explanation: Section 10.5.7: '... an unserviceable light shall not be permitted adjacent to another unserviceable light, except in a barrette or a crossbar where two adjacent unserviceable lights may be permitted.'

question_text: What is the recommended action when the runway surface friction characteristics fall below the minimum friction level specified by the State?

options.B: Corrective maintenance action shall be taken

explanation: Section 10.2.7: 'Corrective maintenance action shall be taken to prevent the runway surface friction characteristics for either the entire runway or a portion thereof from falling below a minimum friction level specified by the State.'

question_text: In Appendix 1, for solid state light sources (LEDs), what is the green boundary for the colour green?
explanation: Appendix 1, section 2.3.1 c): Green - Yellow boundary: x = 0.310.

question_text: According to Appendix 2, for a runway centre line light with 30 m longitudinal spacing (white light), what is the minimum average intensity?
explanation: Appendix 2, Figure A2-6: Minimum average intensity is 5 000 cd (shown on diagram).

question_text: In Appendix 2, Figure A2-12, what is the minimum intensity for taxiway centre line lights (15 m spacing) for RVR less than 350 m?
explanation: Appendix 2, Figure A2-12: Minimum intensity is 20 cd (labelled on diagram).

question_text: True or False: In Appendix 4, the stroke width for a legend height of 400 mm is 64 mm.
explanation: Appendix 4, section 3: Stroke width for legend height 400 mm is 64 mm (table: 200 mm -> 32 mm, 300 mm -> 48 mm, 400 mm -> 64 mm).

question_text: According to Appendix 5, for a medium-intensity dual obstacle lighting system Type A/Type B, which lights are used when background luminescence is below 50 cd/m² (night)?

options.B: Medium-intensity Type B and low-intensity Type B

explanation: Appendix 5, Figure A5-4 note: Medium-intensity obstacle light Type B < 50 cd/m², Low-intensity obstacle light Type B < 50 cd/m² are used at night.

question_text: In Attachment A, what is the recommended maximum spacing between unserviceability area lights per 7.5 m of peripheral distance?

options.A: At least one light for each 5 m
options.B: At least one light for each 7.5 m
options.C: At least one light for each 10 m
options.D: At least one light for each 15 m
options.E: At least one light for each 20 m
options.F: At least one light for each 30 m

explanation: Attachment A, section 13: 'At least one light should be installed for each 7.5 m of peripheral distance of the area.'

question_text: According to Attachment A, section 20 (ARIWS), what should flight crews do when they see red lights from an autonomous runway incursion warning system?
explanation: Attachment A, section 20.2.1: 'Pilots are presented with a globally consistent signal which means 'STOP IMMEDIATELY' and must be taught to react accordingly. Likewise, pilots receiving an ATS clearance to take-off or cross a runway, and seeing the red light array, must STOP and advise ATS that they aborted/stopped because of the red lights.'

question_text: In Attachment A, section 21 (taxiway design to minimize runway incursions), what is recommended regarding the centre line of an entrance taxiway relative to the runway centre line?

options.A: It should be parallel to the runway centre line
options.B: It should be perpendicular to the runway centre line, where possible
options.F: It should be offset by 10 m

explanation: Attachment A, section 21.2: 'The centre line of an entrance taxiway should be perpendicular to the runway centre line, where possible.'
topic: Taxiway design for runway incursion prevention

question_text: What is the maximum allowable tire pressure for code 'Z' in the ACN-PCN method (applicable until 27 November 2024)?
explanation: Section 2.6.6 c): Low: pressure limited to 0.50 MPa, code Z.

question_text: For a precision approach runway category I, what is the maximum allowable tire pressure category code for 'High' pressure in the ACR-PCR method (applicable as of 28 November 2024)?
explanation: Section 2.6.6 c) (applicable as of 28 November 2024): Maximum allowable tire pressure category: Code W: Unlimited; Code X: High (1.75 MPa); Code Y: Medium (1.25 MPa); Code Z: Low (0.50 MPa).

question_text: What is the recommended distance from the runway centre line for a runway-holding position at a taxiway/runway intersection for a precision approach runway code number 4, at elevations not exceeding 700 m?
explanation: Table 3-2 (referenced in section 3.12.6) specifies distances. For precision approach runway code number 4, the distance is 90 m. Section 3.12.7 provides adjustments for elevations above 700 m, but the base is 90 m.

question_text: What is the minimum clearance distance between an aircraft on an aircraft stand and any adjacent building, aircraft on another stand, or other object for code letter F?
explanation: Section 3.13.6 table: Code letter F clearance is 7.5 m.

question_text: What is the description of the conical surface in Annex 14 Volume I?

options.A: A surface located in a horizontal plane above an aerodrome
options.B: A surface sloping upwards and outwards from the periphery of the inner horizontal surface
options.C: An inclined plane preceding the threshold
options.D: A surface along the side of the strip sloping upwards and outwards
options.E: An inclined plane located after the threshold
options.F: A surface beyond the end of a runway for take-off

explanation: Section 4.1.1: 'Conical surface. A surface sloping upwards and outwards from the periphery of the inner horizontal surface.'

question_text: What is the slope of the approach surface for a precision approach runway category I with code number 4?
explanation: Table 4-1: For precision approach runway (categories I, II, III) code number 4, the slope of the first section of the approach surface is 2% (second section slope is 2.5% for some categories, but the first section slope for code number 4 is 2%).

question_text: True or False: The inner horizontal surface height for a non-instrument runway with code number 4 is 100 m according to Table 4-1.
explanation: Table 4-1: For non-instrument runway code number 4, inner horizontal height is 100 m.

question_text: For a non-precision approach runway, what is the divergence (each side) of the approach surface?
explanation: Table 4-1: For non-precision approach runway (all code numbers), divergence each side is 15%.

question_text: What is the final width of the take-off climb surface for code number 3 or 4 when the intended track does not include changes of heading greater than 15 degrees?
explanation: Table 4-2: For code number 3 or 4, final width is 1 200 m (or 1 800 m if changes of heading greater than 15° for operations in IMC, VMC by night). The question specifies 'not greater than 15°', so 1 200 m.

question_text: According to Chapter 5, what is the purpose of a wind direction indicator?

options.B: To give a clear indication of the direction of the surface wind and a general indication of the wind speed

explanation: Section 5.1.1.3: '...constructed so that it gives a clear indication of the direction of the surface wind and a general indication of the wind speed.'

question_text: What is the minimum length of a signal area?
explanation: Section 5.1.4.2: 'The signal area shall be an even horizontal surface at least 9 m square.' So minimum side length is 9 m.

question_text: What is the colour of the landing 'T' when required for use at night?

options.B: Either illuminated or outlined by white lights

explanation: Section 5.1.2.3: 'Where required for use at night the landing 'T' shall either be illuminated or outlined by white lights.'

question_text: What is the minimum colour intensity required for a signalling lamp for daytime use?
explanation: Section 5.1.3.3: 'When the signalling lamp is intended for use in the daytime the intensity of the coloured light should be not less than 6000 cd.'

question_text: True or False: Runway designation numbers on a single runway are determined by the nearest one-tenth of the magnetic North when viewed from the direction of approach.
explanation: Section 5.2.2.4: 'On a single runway, dual parallel runways and triple parallel runways the two-digit number shall be the whole number nearest the one-tenth of the magnetic North when viewed from the direction of approach.'

question_text: For a runway with a displaced threshold, how many stripes are required on each side of the centre line of the runway if the runway designation marking is placed within the threshold marking?
explanation: Section 5.2.4.6: 'Where a runway designation marking is placed within a threshold marking there shall be a minimum of three stripes on each side of the centre line of the runway.'

question_text: What is the distance from the threshold to the beginning of the aiming point marking for a runway with landing distance available less than 800 m?
explanation: Table 5-1: For landing distance available less than 800 m, distance from threshold to beginning of marking is 150 m.

question_text: For a paved precision approach runway with code number 2, what is the required minimum number of pairs of touchdown zone markings if the landing distance available is 900 m up to but not including 1 200 m?
explanation: Section 5.2.6.3 table: For landing distance available 900 m up to but not including 1 200 m, pair(s) of markings = 2.

question_text: What is the colour of the mandatory instruction marking inscription?
explanation: Section 5.2.16.6: 'A mandatory instruction marking shall consist of an inscription in white on a red background.'

question_text: What is the minimum viewing area for a taxiway edge marker as viewed by the pilot (recommended)?
explanation: Section 5.5.5.4: 'The marked surface as viewed by the pilot should be a rectangle and should have a minimum viewing area of 150 cm².'

question_text: What is the recommended height of boundary markers in the form of a cone?
explanation: Section 5.5.8.3: '...or in the form of a cone not less than 50 cm high and not less than 75 cm in diameter at the base.'

question_text: According to Chapter 6, what is required for vehicles and other mobile objects on the movement area?

options.B: They are obstacles and shall be marked and, if used at night or in low visibility, lighted
options.C: Only if they weigh more than 5000 kg

explanation: Section 6.1.1.1: 'Vehicles and other mobile objects, excluding aircraft, on the movement area of an aerodrome are obstacles and shall be marked and, if the vehicles and aerodrome are used at night or in conditions of low visibility, lighted, except that aircraft servicing equipment and vehicles used only on aprons may be exempt.'

question_text: According to Chapter 6, what is the requirement for marking and lighting of overhead wires, cables, etc., crossing a river, waterway, valley or highway?

options.A: They should be marked and their supporting towers marked and lighted if an aeronautical study indicates they could constitute a hazard
options.B: They must always be marked and lighted regardless of hazard
options.C: They require no marking or lighting
options.D: Only the wires need to be marked
options.E: Only the supporting towers need to be lighted

explanation: Section 6.2.5.1 and 6.2.5.2, along with 6.1.1.10 and 6.1.2.3, indicate that overhead wires, cables, etc., should be marked and their supporting towers marked and lighted if an aeronautical study indicates they could constitute a hazard to aircraft.

question_text: For a wind turbine with overall height between 150 m and 315 m, what additional obstacle lighting is required beyond the medium-intensity light on the nacelle?

options.A: A second light serving as an alternate, and an intermediate level at half the nacelle height of at least three low-intensity Type E lights
options.B: High-intensity lights at the top only
options.D: Only a second light on the nacelle
options.F: Flashing red lights on the blades

explanation: Section 6.2.4.3 e) ii) and iii): For wind turbines from 150 m to 315 m in overall height, in addition to the medium-intensity light installed on the nacelle, a second light serving as an alternate should be provided, and an intermediate level at half the nacelle height of at least three low-intensity Type E lights should be provided.

question_text: What is the recommended action for a temporary ramp during runway pavement overlay regarding the entire width of the runway?

options.B: The entire width of the runway should be overlaid during each work session

explanation: Section 10.4.3: 'The entire width of the runway should be overlaid during each work session.'

question_text: In Appendix 3, mandatory instruction markings on pavements are formed as if shadowed from characters of an equivalent elevated sign by what factor?
explanation: Appendix 3, Note 3: 'The mandatory instruction markings and information markings on pavements are formed as if shadowed (i.e., stretched) from the characters of an equivalent elevated sign by a factor of 2.5 as shown in Figure A3-1.'

question_text: In Appendix 4, what is the minimum face height for a sign with legend height of 400 mm?
explanation: Appendix 4, section 10: Legend height 400 mm -> Face height (min) 600 mm.

question_text: According to Attachment A, section 2 (clearways and stopways), what is the relationship between the decision speed and the accelerate-stop distance available?

options.A: A lower decision speed requires a longer accelerate-stop distance available
options.B: A higher decision speed is selected as the accelerate-stop distance available increases
options.C: Decision speed is fixed regardless of accelerate-stop distance
options.D: Decision speed decreases as accelerate-stop distance increases
options.F: Decision speed is determined by wind speed

explanation: Attachment A, section 2.4: 'Normally, a higher decision speed is selected as the accelerate-stop distance available increases.'

question_text: In Attachment A, section 4.3 (radio altimeter operating area), what is the recommended width of the area when special circumstances warrant a reduction?
explanation: Attachment A, section 4.3: 'When special circumstances so warrant, the width may be reduced to no less than 60 m if an aeronautical study indicates that such reduction would not affect the safety of operations of aircraft.' Wait, the text says: 'The area should be symmetrical about the extended centre line, 120 m wide. When special circumstances so warrant, the width may be reduced to no less than 60 m...' Actually re-reading: 'This area should be symmetrical about the extended centre line, 120 m wide. When special circumstances so warrant, the width may be reduced to no less than 60 m...' So the answer is 60 m, not 30. Correction: The reduced width is not less than 60 m. Options include 60 m as B. Let me adjust the correct answer accordingly.

question_text: According to Attachment A, section 5 (runway surface evenness), what is the maximum acceptable height for a step type bump at zero length?
explanation: Attachment A, section 5.3: 'The maximum tolerable step type bump ... is simply the bump height corresponding to zero bump length at the upper end of the tolerable region of the roughness criteria of Figure A-3. The bump height at this location is 1.75 cm.'

question_text: In Attachment A, section 6 (runway condition report), what is the purpose of the runway condition code (RWYCC)?

options.A: To classify the airport's maintenance level
options.B: To permit an operational aeroplane performance calculation by the flight crew
options.D: To set speed limits on taxiways

explanation: Attachment A, section 6.2 b): 'a unique runway condition code (RWYCC) linking the agreed set of criteria with the aircraft landing and take-off performance table, and related to the braking action experienced and eventually reported by flight crews;' Also section 6.3 refers to correlation with expected braking action.

question_text: According to Attachment A, section 7 (drainage), what is the threshold value between microtexture and macrotexture using the volumetric method?
explanation: Attachment A, section 7.3.11: 'These standards describe the threshold value between microtexture and macrotexture as 0.5 mm.'

question_text: In Attachment A, section 8 (strips), what is the recommended graded width for a precision approach runway with code number 3 or 4?

options.B: 105 m from centre line, reducing to 75 m at both ends

explanation: Attachment A, section 8.3: 'Figure A-4 shows the shape and dimensions of a wider strip that may be considered for such a runway. The portion to be graded extends to a distance of 105 m from the centre line, except that the distance is gradually reduced to 75 m from the centre line at both ends of the strip, for a length of 150 m from the runway end.'

question_text: According to Attachment A, section 9 (runway end safety areas), what is the recommended length of RESA for a precision approach runway where the ILS localizer is the first upstanding obstacle?
explanation: Attachment A, section 9.1: 'On a precision approach runway, the ILS localizer is normally the first upstanding obstacle, and the runway end safety area should extend up to this facility.'

question_text: In Attachment A, section 10 (location of threshold), what is the recommended maximum slope of the obstacle free surface to the threshold for code number 4?
explanation: Attachment A, section 10.2.4: 'the selected position for the threshold should not be such that the obstacle free surface to the threshold is steeper than 3.3 per cent where the code number is 4 or steeper than 5 per cent where the code number is 3.'

question_text: According to Attachment A, section 11 (approach lighting systems), what is the maximum tolerance for the centre line of an approach lighting system relative to the extended runway centre line?
explanation: Attachment A, section 11.2.2: 'The centre line of an approach lighting system should be as coincident as possible with the extended centre line of the runway with a maximum tolerance of ±15'.'

question_text: True or False: In Attachment A, section 17 (rescue and firefighting), the training curriculum for rescue personnel must include instruction in dangerous goods.
explanation: Attachment A, section 17.2 lists 'j) dangerous goods' as part of the training curriculum.

question_text: According to Attachment A, section 19 (overload operations using ACN-PCN method applicable until 27 Nov 2024), for flexible pavements, occasional movements by aircraft with ACN not exceeding what percentage above the reported PCN should not adversely affect the pavement?
explanation: Attachment A, section 19.1.1 a): 'for flexible pavements, occasional movements by aircraft with ACN not exceeding 10 per cent above the reported PCN should not adversely affect the pavement;'

question_text: In Attachment A, section 21 (taxiway design for runway incursion prevention), what is recommended regarding multi-taxiway entrances to a runway?

options.A: They should converge into one wide entrance
options.B: They should be parallel to each other and distinctly separated by an unpaved area
options.C: They should be perpendicular to the runway
options.D: They should be offset by 30 degrees
options.E: They should share a common holding position

explanation: Attachment A, section 21.5: 'Multi-taxiway entrances to a runway should be parallel to each other and should be distinctly separated by an unpaved area.'

question_text: According to Attachment A, section 22 (aerodrome mapping data), what are the two levels of quality for aerodrome mapping databases?
explanation: Attachment A, section 22, reference to Note 2 in 2.1.3: 'Aerodrome mapping databases can be provided at one of two levels of quality — fine or medium.'

question_text: In Attachment B, Figure B-1, what is the slope of the take-off climb surface for the non-instrument runway shown?
explanation: Attachment B, Figure B-1 labels the take-off climb surface for the non-instrument runway with 'Slope 2%'.
image_description: Figure B-1 shows obstacle limitation surfaces with slopes indicated

question_text: According to Annex 14 Volume I, what is the definition of 'Take-off runway'?

options.A: A runway intended for both take-off and landing
options.B: A runway intended for take-off only
options.C: A runway used for emergency take-offs
options.E: A runway equipped with centre line lights
options.F: A runway for turbojet aircraft only

explanation: Section 1.1 Definitions: 'Take-off runway. A runway intended for take-off only.'

question_text: What is the definition of 'Rapid exit taxiway'?

options.A: A taxiway parallel to the runway
options.B: A taxiway connected to a runway at an acute angle designed to allow landing aeroplanes to turn off at higher speeds
options.C: A taxiway used only for emergency exits
options.D: A taxiway with a 90-degree angle to the runway
options.E: A taxiway that bypasses the apron
options.F: A taxiway for departing aircraft only

explanation: Section 1.1 Definitions: 'Rapid exit taxiway. A taxiway connected to a runway at an acute angle and designed to allow landing aeroplanes to turn off at higher speeds than are achieved on other exit taxiways thereby minimizing runway occupancy times.'

question_text: What is the definition of 'Standing water' as a runway surface condition descriptor?

options.A: Water of depth up to 3 mm
options.B: Water of depth greater than 3 mm
options.E: Water that runs off the runway

explanation: Section 1.1 Definitions e) vi): 'Standing water. Water of depth greater than 3 mm.'

question_text: What is the definition of 'Usability factor'?

options.A: The percentage of time during which a runway can be used for landing only
options.B: The percentage of time during which the use of a runway or system of runways is not restricted because of the crosswind component
options.C: The ratio of take-offs to landings
options.E: The percentage of time the runway is free of ice

explanation: Section 1.1 Definitions: 'Usability factor. The percentage of time during which the use of a runway or system of runways is not restricted because of the crosswind component.'

question_text: According to section 2.5.5, to what precision shall the geographical coordinates of obstacles in Area 2 and Area 3 be reported?

options.A: Degrees, minutes, seconds and hundredths of seconds
options.B: Degrees, minutes, seconds and tenths of seconds
options.F: Degrees, minutes, seconds and thousandths of seconds

explanation: Section 2.5.5: 'The geographical coordinates of obstacles in Area 2 (the part within the aerodrome boundary) and in Area 3 shall be measured and reported to the aeronautical information services authority in degrees, minutes, seconds and tenths of seconds.'

question_text: For a non-precision approach runway, what is the distance from the threshold to the inner edge of the approach surface?
explanation: Table 4-1: For non-precision approach runways, distance from threshold to inner edge is 60 m for all code numbers.

question_text: What is the slope of the transitional surface for a non-instrument runway with code number 4?
explanation: Table 4-1: For non-instrument runway code number 4, transitional slope is 20% (the table shows 20% for non-instrument code numbers 3 and 4, and 14.3% for code numbers 1 and 2).

question_text: According to Chapter 5, what is the minimum number of lights in a threshold wing bar?
explanation: Section 5.3.10.8: 'Each wing bar shall be formed by at least five lights extending at least 10 m outward from, and at right angles to, the line of the runway edge lights...'

question_text: For a taxiway intended for use in RVR conditions less than 350 m, what is the maximum longitudinal spacing of taxiway centre line lights on a straight section?
explanation: Section 5.3.17.13 c): 'on a taxiway intended for use in RVR conditions of less than a value of 350 m, the longitudinal spacing should not exceed 15 m.'

question_text: What is the required minimum intensity of unserviceability lights?
explanation: Section 7.4.4: 'In no case shall the intensity be less than 10 cd of red light.'

question_text: For a stop bar at a runway-holding position, what is the maximum time for an indication of a change in operational status to be provided (recommended)?
explanation: Section 8.3.3: 'Where a change in the operational status of lights has occurred, an indication should be provided within two seconds for a stop bar at a runway-holding position and within five seconds for all other types of visual aids.'

question_text: According to Chapter 9, what is the role of the emergency operations centre?

options.B: To provide overall coordination and general direction of the response to an emergency
options.C: To manage air traffic during an emergency

explanation: Section 9.1.8: 'The emergency operations centre should be a part of the aerodrome facilities and should be responsible for the overall coordination and general direction of the response to an emergency.'

question_text: What is the minimum number of rescue and firefighting vehicles for aerodrome category 10?
explanation: Section 9.2.40 table: Aerodrome category 10 requires 3 rescue and firefighting vehicles.

question_text: True or False: The driver of a radio-equipped vehicle must establish two-way radio communication with the aerodrome control tower before entering the apron only, not the manoeuvring area.
explanation: Section 9.7.5: 'The driver of a radio-equipped vehicle shall establish satisfactory two-way radio communication with the aerodrome control tower before entering the manoeuvring area and with the appropriate designated authority before entering the apron.'

question_text: According to Chapter 10, what is the minimum serviceability objective for runway edge lights on a precision approach runway category II or III?
explanation: Section 10.5.7 a) 4): '95 per cent of the lights are serviceable in each of the following particular significant elements: ... 4) runway edge lights;'

question_text: For a stop bar provided at a runway-holding position used with a runway intended for operations in RVR less than 350 m, what is the serviceability objective regarding unserviceable lights?

options.A: No more than one light unserviceable
options.B: No more than two lights unserviceable, and two adjacent lights not remaining unserviceable unless spacing is less than specified

explanation: Section 10.5.8: 'The system of preventive maintenance employed for a stop bar ... shall have the following objectives: a) no more than two lights will remain unserviceable; and b) two adjacent lights will not remain unserviceable unless the light spacing is significantly less than that specified.'

question_text: In Appendix 1, for filament-type lights, what is the yellow boundary for green?
explanation: Appendix 1, section 2.1.1 c): Green - Yellow boundary: x = 0.360 - 0.080y.

question_text: In Appendix 2, Figure A2-3 (threshold light), what is the minimum average intensity for green light?
explanation: Appendix 2, Figure A2-3: Minimum average intensity is 10 000 cd (labelled on the diagram).

question_text: According to Appendix 4, what is the minimum character height for a runway exit sign on runway code number 1 or 2?
explanation: Appendix 4, section 1 table: For runway code number 1 or 2, mandatory instruction sign (runway exit sign falls under mandatory instruction) minimum character height is 300 mm. Specifically, 'Runway exit and runway vacated signs' are listed separately? The table shows: Mandatory instruction sign: 300 mm for code 1 or 2; Runway exit and runway vacated signs: 300 mm; Other signs: 200 mm. So runway exit sign is 300 mm.

question_text: In Appendix 5, for a medium-intensity flashing-white obstacle lighting system (Type A) on a structure of height 150 m, how many levels of lights are shown?
explanation: Appendix 5, Figure A5-1: For a structure height of 150 m, lights are placed at top, at 105 m, and at 45 m? Actually the figure shows levels: top, at 105 m, and at 45 m? For 150 m, the diagram indicates 3 levels: 0? Let's see: The figure has a scale. Typically, for 150 m, there are lights at the top, at 105 m, and at 45 m (or ground). But the recommended spacing is 105 m maximum. For 150 m, you would have top, then one intermediate at 105 m, and ground? That's 3 levels. The correct answer is 3.

question_text: In Attachment A, section 1.1.2, what factor is NOT listed as requiring reduction of maximum crosswind component?
explanation: Attachment A, section 1.1.2 b) lists factors: handling characteristics, prevalence and nature of gusts, prevalence and nature of turbulence, availability of a secondary runway, width of runways, runway surface conditions, strength of wind. Aircraft colour is not mentioned.

question_text: According to Attachment A, section 3, when a runway has a displaced threshold, which declared distance is affected?

options.B: LDA for approaches made to that threshold

explanation: Attachment A, section 3.5: 'If a runway has a displaced threshold, then the LDA will be reduced by the distance the threshold is displaced, as shown in Figure A-1 (D). A displaced threshold affects only the LDA for approaches made to that threshold; all declared distances for operations in the reciprocal direction are unaffected.'

question_text: In Attachment A, section 5.3, what is the acceptable surface irregularity height for a length of 45 m?
explanation: Attachment A, section 5.3 table: For length 45 m, acceptable surface irregularity height is 8.5 cm.

question_text: According to Attachment A, section 7.3.9, what is the texture depth for ESDU classification C?
explanation: Attachment A, section 7.3.9 table: Classification C texture depths: 0.25 – 0.50 mm.

question_text: In Attachment A, section 11.3.4, for an ILS localizer located 300 m from the threshold, what is the maximum height it may extend above the plane of the approach lighting system?
explanation: Attachment A, section 11.3.4: 'the screen will be permitted to extend above the plane of the approach lighting system by 10 × 15 = 150 cm maximum' (since 300/30 = 10, times 15 cm = 150 cm).

question_text: According to Attachment A, section 14 (RETILs), what is the recommended roll-out speed until the first RETIL (three-light barrette) is reached?
explanation: Attachment A, section 14.3: 'A roll-out speed of 60 knots until the first RETIL (three-light barrette) is reached is seen as the optimum.'

question_text: In Attachment A, section 18 (operators of vehicles), what knowledge should vehicle operators have as appropriate to their function?

options.A: Aerodrome geography, signs, markings, lights, radiotelephone procedures, ATC terms, airport rules, and specialist functions

explanation: Attachment A, section 18.1 lists: geography, aerodrome signs, markings and lights, radiotelephone operating procedures, terms and phrases used in aerodrome control including ICAO spelling alphabet, rules of air traffic services, airport rules and procedures, and specialist functions as required.

question_text: According to Attachment A, section 19.1.1 (applicable as of 28 November 2024 for ACR-PCR), what is the allowable overload percentage for flexible and rigid pavements?
explanation: Attachment A, section 19.1.1 a) (applicable as of 28 November 2024): 'for flexible and rigid pavements, occasional movements by aircraft with ACR not exceeding 10 per cent above the reported PCR should not adversely affect the pavement;'

question_text: In Attachment A, section 20 (ARIWS), in case the system fails in the illuminated condition, what is recommended?

options.B: Procedures should be developed by ATS and/or aerodrome operator
options.D: The system must be repaired immediately on site

explanation: Attachment A, section 20.2.4: 'Procedures should be developed to address the circumstance where the system fails in the illuminated condition. It will be up to the ATS and/or aerodrome operator to establish those procedures depending on their own circumstances.'

question_text: According to Annex 14 Volume I, what is the definition of 'Touchdown zone'?

options.A: The first 300 m of the runway from the threshold
options.B: The portion of a runway, beyond the threshold, where it is intended landing aeroplanes first contact the runway
options.C: The area with touchdown zone markings
options.D: The final 300 m before the runway end
options.E: The section of runway with highest friction
options.F: The area used for take-off roll

explanation: Section 1.1 Definitions: 'Touchdown zone. The portion of a runway, beyond the threshold, where it is intended landing aeroplanes first contact the runway.'

question_text: What is the definition of 'Holdover time'?

options.A: The time an aircraft can hold on a taxiway
options.B: The estimated time the anti-icing fluid will prevent formation of ice and frost on protected surfaces
options.C: The time required for de-icing to be effective
options.D: The time between de-icing and take-off
options.E: The time a runway is closed for snow removal
options.F: The time an aircraft waits for departure clearance

explanation: Section 1.1 Definitions: 'Holdover time. The estimated time the anti-icing fluid (treatment) will prevent the formation of ice and frost and the accumulation of snow on the protected (treated) surfaces of an aeroplane.'
